---
title: "Edge cases in the three-phase kernel"
subtitle: "What `_pattern!` / `_fill!` actually do at the boundaries"
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    geometry:
      - margin=2cm
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{tikz}
        \usetikzlibrary{positioning,arrows.meta,calc}
---

## Setup

The three-phase kernel decomposes the column axis `[cmin, cmax]` into
three runs of constant active count:

| phase       | columns               | `active`                          | rows                             |
|-------------|-----------------------|-----------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| Left ramp   | `[cmin, c_LR − 1]`    | `max(0, c − rmin − δ_lo + 1)`     | `1, 2, …, active(c)`             |
| Interior    | `[c_LR, c_RR]`        | `Leff = δ_hi − δ_lo + 1` const.   | `r0 … r0 + Leff − 1`, stride 1   |
| Right ramp  | `[c_RR + 1, cmax]`    | `max(0, δ_hi − c + rmax + 1)`     | `r0 … r0 + active − 1`, stride 1 |

with `δ_lo = max(O, cmin − rmax)`, `δ_hi = min(O + L − 1, cmax − rmin)`,
`c_LR = max(cmin, rmin + δ_hi)`, `c_RR = min(cmax, rmax + δ_lo)`,
`r0 = c − δ_hi − rmin + 1`. Inside each phase the inner loop walks `δ`
high → low so the emitted rows ascend per column (CSC sortedness without
sort). The interior uses closed-form
`cur(c) = cur_int_0 + Leff * (c − c_LR)` — column writes are independent.

This document covers the three boundary scenarios that the kernel must
handle correctly: the empty-operator early return, the off-mesh column
tails (where `max(0, active)` clipping kicks in), and the
`L = length(row) + 1` boundary (where the interior phase collapses).

## The empty operator: `δ_lo > δ_hi`

If `δ_lo > δ_hi` no offset reaches any valid row from any valid column —
the matrix is structurally all-zero. The kernel takes an early return:

```julia
if δ_lo > δ_hi
    resize!(rowval, 0)
    for c in cmin:cmax
        colptr[c - cmin + 2] = 1
    end
    return
end
```

`rowval` is shrunk to length zero, and every `colptr[2..n+1]` is set to
`1` so `colptr[j+1] − colptr[j] = 0` (zero nonzeros) for every column.
Triggering examples:

- Stencil sits entirely above the row range: `O = 10, L = 2` on
  `row = 1:5, col = 1:5` gives `δ_lo = 10, δ_hi = 4`.
- `col` and `row` are far apart with small offsets: `row = 1:5,
  col = 100:100, O = -1, L = 3` gives `δ_lo = 95, δ_hi = -1`.

## Off-mesh column tails: why `max(0, active)`

The non-empty active set lives on column range
`[rmin + δ_lo, rmax + δ_hi]`. When `col` extends outside this range,
some columns at the extremes carry no entries (just `colptr` markers).
The ramp `active` formulas

    active_L(c) = c − rmin − δ_lo + 1     (left ramp)
    active_R(c) = δ_hi − c + rmax + 1     (right ramp)

go **negative** in those tails: at the left, when `c < rmin + δ_lo`;
at the right, when `c > rmax + δ_hi`. Letting `cur += active` with a
negative `active` would corrupt the slot counter. The fix is to clip:

```julia
active = max(0, c - rmin - δ_lo + 1)   # left ramp
active = max(0, δ_hi - c + rmax + 1)   # right ramp
```

For zero `active` the inner loop `for i in 0:(active − 1)` is empty,
no `rowval` writes happen, `cur` is unchanged, and only `colptr` is
written (pointing to the running `cur`).

```{=latex}
\begin{figure}[h]
\centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1.0, font=\small]
  \draw[->, thick] (-0.3, 0) -- (10.3, 0) node[right] {$c$};

  \foreach \c/\lbl in {0/{$c_{\min}$},2/2,4/{$r_{\max}{+}\delta_{hi}$},5/5,6/6,7/7,9/{$c_{\max}$}} {
    \draw (\c, 0.08) -- (\c, -0.08);
    \node[below=2pt, font=\scriptsize] at (\c, -0.08) {\lbl};
  }

  \draw[blue!50, line width=4pt, opacity=0.35] (0, 0) -- (4, 0);
  \node[blue!60!black, font=\scriptsize] at (2, 0.45) {non-empty active};

  \draw[red!60!black, line width=4pt, opacity=0.35] (4.05, 0) -- (9, 0);
  \node[red!70!black, font=\scriptsize] at (6.5, 0.45) {off-mesh right tail (active $\le 0$)};

  \node[font=\scriptsize, align=center] at (5, -0.95)
    {right-ramp formula returns $\le 0$ here;\\\texttt{max(0, active)} clips, \texttt{cur} unchanged};
\end{tikzpicture}
\caption{Off-mesh right tail: columns past $r_{\max}+\delta_{\hi}$ produce no entries; \texttt{max(0, active)} keeps the slot counter consistent.}
\end{figure}
```

Concrete example: `row = 1:5, col = 1:100, offsets = SUnitRange(-1, 1)`
gives `δ_hi = 1`, so columns 7..100 lie past `rmax + δ_hi = 6` and the
right-ramp `active = 1 − c + 5 + 1` is `0, −1, −2, …` for those columns.

## The `L = length(row) + 1` boundary

The runtime guard `L − 1 ≤ length(row[1])` (= `m`) is the *exact*
correctness boundary of the three-phase kernel. The interesting case is
the equality `L = m + 1`.

At that boundary, with no trimming, `Leff = m + 1` and

    c_LR − c_RR = (rmin + δ_hi) − (rmax + δ_lo) = Leff − m = 1,

so `c_LR = c_RR + 1`. The interior range `c_LR:c_RR` is empty (Julia
handles this with a zero-iteration `for` loop), and the left ramp
covers `[cmin, c_RR]` while the right ramp covers
`[c_RR + 1, cmax] = [c_LR, cmax]`. The two ramps **tile** the column
range without gap.

At the last left-ramp column `c = c_RR`, `active = m` (the row capacity);
at the first right-ramp column `c = c_LR`, `active = m` again. The
interior — which would have wanted all `m + 1` offsets simultaneously
active — is unreachable by construction: no column has that much row
range available.

Beyond the boundary (`L = m + 2`), `c_LR > c_RR + 1`, and the columns
in `(c_RR, c_LR)` would need a fourth **saturated middle** phase with
both bounds dynamic and `active = m`. That phase is deliberately out
of scope; the runtime guard prevents callers from entering this regime.

## Why `cur` stays consistent across all three cases

The kernel maintains the invariant `cur == total nonzeros written so far`
at every column boundary. Each of the boundary scenarios above preserves
the invariant:

- **Empty operator**: `cur = 1` throughout; `colptr` filled with `1`s.
- **Off-mesh tail** (clipped `active = 0`): inner loop empty;
  `cur += 0`; `colptr[c − cmin + 2] = cur`.
- **`L = m + 1` boundary** (empty interior): interior loop has zero
  iterations; `cur = cur_int_0 + Leff * max(0, c_RR − c_LR + 1) =
  cur_int_0 + 0`. The `max(0, …)` is needed because `c_RR − c_LR + 1`
  is `0` (range is `c_LR:c_RR = c_LR:(c_LR − 1)`, empty).

Together, these three mechanisms — early return, `max(0, active)`
clipping, and `max(0, c_RR − c_LR + 1)` after the interior — make the
kernel total over the full allowed input space `L ≤ length(row) + 1`.
